lessons.title: How the WNBA is different from the NBA
lessons.subtitle: Court, clock, and gameplay differences explained
lessons.intro: Same game, different rules. The W has tweaked the rulebook in ways that actually shift the entire flavor of play. Here's what changes when you watch.

sections.text: The court is the same size, but the 3-point line is different

The court dimensions are identical to the NBA. But here's the thing: the WNBA 3-point line is **closer** — about 1.25 feet closer at the wings than the NBA's. This might sound small, but it fundamentally changes spacing, offensive strategy, and why you see so much more ball movement and perimeter passing in the W. Teams can't just camp at the NBA 3-line and expect the same spacing.
The clock and quarters

WNBA games are 40 minutes (four 10-minute quarters), compared to the NBA's 48 minutes. This makes the league feel faster, more intense, more consistent. There's less foul trouble because fewer minutes means fewer opportunities to foul out. Quarters are shorter, so every possession hits harder.

sections.def: The line beyond which a made basket counts as 3 points. The W's line is closer than the NBA's, changing the entire offensive landscape.

sections.text: Fouls and physicality

items: Players foul out after **6 fouls**, same as the NBA
Flagrant fouls are called more strictly in the W — loose-ball physicality gets whistled
Defensive 3-second violations (zone defense rules) exist but are called with less frequency
Traveling and carrying violations are strict — the W cracks down on handles-heavy, NBA-style dribbling nonsense

sections.title: Why this matters
sections.body: The stricter officiating on certain fouls, combined with the shorter clock, means the W rewards **efficiency, teamwork, and clean fundamentals**. You won't see as much iso-ball and muscle-through-it play. It's possession basketball.

sections.text: No defensive 3-in-the-key (technically)

The W eliminated the strict 3-second defensive violation that the NBA enforces. This allows centers and forwards to camp the paint longer, which is why rebounding and post-play feel more prominent in the W than in modern NBA ball.

sections.def: Excessive or violent contact. In the W, flagrant fouls result in free throws and possession, and two flagrant fouls get you ejected.
Accumulating 6 personal fouls removes a player from the game. In a 40-minute game, foul trouble hits faster than the NBA.

sections.text: The W's rulebook prioritizes skill and spacing over physicality.

prompts: Why does a closer 3-point line change the way teams play offense?

Are WNBA players 'fouling out' faster than NBA players because of the rules?

lessons.title: Positions, style of play, what makes the W feel different
lessons.subtitle: Guard, forward, center — and why the W plays smaller
lessons.intro: The W has the same 5 positions as the NBA, but the positions mean something slightly different. And the overall style of play is distinct in ways you'll notice immediately — more spacing, more movement, more three-ball.

sections.text: The five positions

items: **Point Guard (PG)**: The floor general, ball handler, passer. Smaller, quicker, runs the offense
**Shooting Guard (SG)**: Off-ball scorer, perimeter specialist, often the secondary playmaker
**Small Forward (SF)**: Wing player, can play inside or outside, high versatility
**Power Forward (PF)**: Post player, rebounder, defender, increasingly a stretch/3-point threat
**Center (C)**: Inside anchor, rim protector, rebounder, traditionally the biggest player

sections.text: Position-less basketball is real in the W

Here's what you notice: the W skews **smaller and more skilled** than the NBA. You'll see guards initiating offense from the wing, forwards running pick-and-roll, and centers stretching to the 3-point line. Teams value **versatility, footwork, and IQ** over size. A 6'2" forward might guard a 6'4" power forward because the shorter player has better feet and discipline.

sections.title: Why the W plays a different game
sections.body: The shorter 3-point line, the 40-minute format, and the emphasis on spacing push teams toward smaller lineups, more perimeter ball movement, and less back-to-basket play than you see in the NBA. It's spread-out, skill-first basketball.

sections.text: What the W's style actually looks like

items: **Ball movement**: Quick, snappy passes that reward spacing and footwork
**Perimeter-heavy offense**: More 3-pointers, more off-ball action, less iso-ball
**Defensive fundamentals**: Tight closeouts, switch-heavy defense, fewer 'just guard him physically' tactics
**Pace**: Deliberate, controlled tempo — not necessarily slower, but more methodical
**Transition**: Fast breaks are devastating but not excessive — the W rewards half-court execution

sections.text: The athlete you should watch

If you want to understand the W's style, watch **Sabrina Ionescu** (New York Liberty). She's a 6'2" guard who orchestrates the offense, shoots threes from 35+ feet, and makes high-IQ passes that feel like *art*. She's the physical embodiment of how the W values skill, spacing, and basketball IQ over pure athleticism.

sections.def: Using players based on skill and matchup rather than traditional position size. In the W, a guard might play forward, and a center might shoot threes.
A big player who can shoot from beyond the 3-point line, opening up spacing and forcing defenses to adjust.
Defending by swapping matchups on screens instead of fighting through them. Requires IQ and footwork, not just size.
An offensive play where one player (usually a guard) uses a teammate's screen to get open, then either drives or passes. The most common offensive set in basketball.

sections.text: The W is guard-friendly, perimeter-friendly basketball.

prompts: Why does a smaller player sometimes defend a larger player in the W?

lessons.title: The stars to know in 2026
lessons.subtitle: A guide to the league's biggest names and what makes them lethal
lessons.intro: 2026 is stacked. These are the players you'll hear about constantly — the MVPs, the up-and-comers, the playoff-game closers. Get to know them now.

sections.text: A'ja Wilson — the GOAT conversation

A'ja plays for the **Las Vegas Aces** and is a **4-time MVP**. Yes, four. She's an elite two-way player — unstoppable in the post, dominant defender, and the model of consistency. A'ja is 6'4", has footwork that belies her size, and can take over games in stretches where the Aces need a bucket. She's the gravity around which her team orbits.
Caitlin Clark — the generational shooter

Caitlin plays for the **Indiana Fever** and entered the league in 2025 as the #1 pick after dominating at Iowa. She's a 6'1" guard with a three-level scoring game and range that extends to 40 feet. She's the leading scorer in the league and plays with an edge — she competes on every possession. She's not just a shooter; she's a **point guard who can create for others** while carrying the scoring load.
Angel Reese — the rebound queen

Angel just got traded to the **Atlanta Dream** after a stint in Chicago. She's a 6'3" power forward who is an *absolute monster* on the glass — she gets rebounds that don't seem physically possible. She's aggressive, fun, and one of the best defenders in the league. Her energy is infectious; she makes her teammates better by sheer will and rebounding.
The deep New York Liberty bench

items: **Sabrina Ionescu**: 6'2" guard, 35+ foot three-pointer, the offensive conductor. She runs the Liberty's offense with a precision that feels surgical
**Breanna Stewart**: 6'4" forward, playoff-mode forward, defender, rebounder. She's at her best when games get tight and the skill-level demands peak

sections.text: Paige Bueckers — the UConn legacy

Paige plays for the **Dallas Wings** after being the #1 pick in 2025. She's a 5'11" combo guard from the UConn dynasty, which means she has championship DNA and an elite work ethic. She's a scorer first, defender second, and her game is built on mid-range pull-ups and quick decisions. In 2026, she's proving she can be a primary ball-handler in the WNBA.
Napheesa Collier — the quiet MVP candidate

Napheesa plays for the **Minnesota Lynx** and is one of the best two-way players in the league. She's also a **co-founder of Unrivaled**, the off-season 3-on-3 league that's changing the basketball calendar. She's 6'0", a relentless defender, a smart passer, and a reliable scorer. She doesn't always get the hype, but she gets results.
Cameron Brink — the comeback story

Cameron plays for the **LA Sparks** and is coming back from a serious ACL injury that sidelined her in 2025. She's a 6'4" forward, Stanford grad, and one of the most dynamic athletes in the league. When healthy, she's a lob-catcher, a defender, and a presence inside. Her 2026 season is about proving her body can handle WNBA minutes.
Kelsey Plum and Arike Ogunbowale — the scorers you forgot about

items: **Kelsey Plum** (Las Vegas Aces): 5'8" guard, elite shooter, crafty playmaker. She plays alongside A'ja Wilson and is an off-ball sniper
**Arike Ogunbowale** (Dallas Wings): 5'8" guard, one of the most talented scorers in the league. She's explosive, creative, and doesn't get enough credit

sections.title: The star power is real
sections.body: 2026 is the most star-studded WNBA season in history. You've got four-time MVPs, generational rookies, and rising stars all competing at the same time. Ticket prices are up. Viewership is up. The league is hitting a moment.

sections.def: Most Valuable Player. The award given to the best overall player in the regular season. A'ja Wilson has won it four times.
A player who appears once per era and changes the game — someone like Caitlin Clark, who comes along rarely.
A player who is elite on both offense and defense. A'ja Wilson and Napheesa Collier are textbook two-way stars.
A player (usually a center or power forward) who finishes alley-oop passes with dunks or close-range finishes. Cameron Brink is a natural.

lessons.title: The season, the playoffs, the Finals
lessons.subtitle: How the WNBA year unfolds and what matters
lessons.intro: The WNBA season is compressed, intense, and high-stakes. Here's how it works — from opening tip to championship.

sections.text: The regular season: May 16 – September

The 2026 regular season starts **May 16** and runs through early September. Each team plays **40 games** — fewer than the NBA, which makes every game feel consequential. There's no padding; losses stack up quickly. The season is shorter, so there's less room for slumps or injuries. Hot teams stay hot. Cold teams can't catch up.
What happens during the season: The Commissioner's Cup

Midway through the season, the league runs the **Commissioner's Cup** — a mid-season tournament where teams earn bonus points and the winning team (chosen by fan votes and league performance) gets prize money. It's WNBA's version of in-season play, and it's become a big deal. Teams take it seriously.
Playoff seeding: top 8 teams make it

items: Top 8 teams in the league standings qualify for the playoffs
No conference split — it's a pure standings race, East vs. West doesn't matter
Teams are seeded 1-8 based on regular season record
High seeds get home-court advantage (best-of-3 or best-of-5 series)

sections.text: The playoffs and Finals: August through September

The playoffs start in late August with the first round (best-of-3). Seeds 1-2 get a bye and play in the second round. The Finals are typically a best-of-5 series, played in late September or early October. The winning team is crowned **WNBA Champions** and their city gets a parade.

sections.title: The Finals are the moment
sections.body: The WNBA Finals are electric. It's the culmination of a short, intense season. Games are decisive, individual performances matter, and the pressure is maximum. This is championship-or-nothing basketball.

sections.text: Awards and recognition

items: **MVP**: Most Valuable Player, regular season
**DPOY**: Defensive Player of the Year
**ROY**: Rookie of the Year
**All-WNBA Teams**: First, Second, Third team recognition
**All-Defensive Teams**: Recognition for perimeter and frontcourt defense

sections.def: The 40-game slate where all teams compete for playoff positioning. Runs May-early September.
A mid-season tournament within the regular season that gives extra incentive and prize money. Fans and league play a role in determining winners.
The rankings of the top 8 teams. Seed 1 plays the highest remaining seed, etc. No conference split in the W — it's pure standings.
The championship series between the two best teams remaining after the playoffs. Usually best-of-5, played in late September.

sections.text: 40 games. Top 8 teams. One champion. That's the W's arc.

lessons.title: The college pipeline + Unrivaled (off-season league)
lessons.subtitle: How players reach the W, and what happens when the regular season ends
lessons.intro: The WNBA doesn't exist in a vacuum. College basketball feeds the league, and now an off-season circuit called Unrivaled keeps stars playing year-round. Here's the ecosystem.

sections.text: College basketball to the WNBA: the pipeline

The best female college players come into the WNBA through the draft, held each spring. Teams pick in reverse order of standings (worst team picks first, to balance competitive strength). **Iowa, UConn, and Stanford** are traditional powerhouses that send multiple players to the league annually. Caitlin Clark came from Iowa. Paige Bueckers came from UConn. These programs are basically the WNBA minor league.
The WNBA Draft: April each year

The draft is held in April, a few weeks before the season starts. The lottery determines the first few picks; teams can trade draft picks to improve their chances. Top college stars are scouted obsessively — there's real money and prestige in landing a generational talent like Caitlin Clark. The draft class is studied for years.
Unrivaled: the off-season game-changer

**Unrivaled** is a brand-new 3-on-3 off-season league that launched in 2024. Top WNBA stars like **Napheesa Collier** co-founded it to keep players engaged and paid during the off-season (roughly October-April). It's smaller courts, faster games, and different rules — but it's legitimately good basketball and it's pulling viewership. It also competes with international basketball for player talent.
The basketball calendar is now year-round

items: **May-September**: WNBA regular season and playoffs
**October-November**: WNBA Finals conclude, off-season begins
**November-March**: Unrivaled 3-on-3 season, players earn money and stay game-ready
**April**: WNBA Draft, pre-season starts
**May**: WNBA season tip-off

sections.title: Unrivaled changes the game
sections.body: For the first time, WNBA stars can stay home, keep playing, and earn significant money off-season. This is huge for player earnings, brand building, and keeping the sport in the conversation year-round.

sections.text: International basketball: the other option

Some WNBA stars play overseas in off-seasons — Europe and Asia have professional leagues that pay well and offer cultural experiences. But Unrivaled is changing that calculus. Why leave the country when you can play in the US, build your brand, and earn money?

sections.def: Annual event in April where teams select college players to join the league. Top college stars are highly scouted.
The system that determines which of the non-playoff teams gets the first pick. Weighted toward the worst team, but with randomness to discourage tanking.
A 3-on-3 off-season league co-founded by WNBA stars like Napheesa Collier. Runs November-March and pays players to stay game-ready.
The months when the WNBA isn't playing (October-April). Players traditionally played overseas or rested; now many play Unrivaled.

sections.text: The W is now a year-round sport, not a May-September event.
